lessons.title: Welcome and Mindset
lessons.description: Learn the BMH Group service standard and the mindset that keeps seller conversations clear, calm, and human.

content.html:
What you will learn

Learn the BMH Group service standard and the mindset that keeps seller conversations clear, calm, and human.

  • Explain the BMH service standard
  • Put service before pressure
  • Use curiosity and clarity in seller conversations
  • Detach from outcomes while staying accountable

Learner guide

  • The goal is an aligned decision, not a forced yes
  • Listen for the seller's real problem before discussing a solution
  • Treat repetition as practice that creates calm
  • A respectful no is better than a pressured agreement

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Welcome and Mindset

cards.front: How does the BMH Group define its mission beyond just performing a job?
cards.back: To help families overcome difficult situations and move forward with clarity and certainty.

cards.front: What distinguishes the sellers BMH works with from those in traditional real estate?
cards.back: They are often in the middle of hard life moments like foreclosure, loss, or property distress.

cards.front: What is the primary technical 'tool' BMH uses to provide speed and certainty to homeowners?
cards.back: Buying houses for cash in as-is condition.

cards.front: Beyond structure and confidence, what is the ultimate purpose of the provided script?
cards.back: To give a clear path through every conversation.

cards.front: According to the training, when should an exceptional rep set the script aside?
cards.back: When they need to listen, slow down, or be present with the seller.

cards.front: What is the primary goal of a BMH rep during a conversation with a distressed seller?
cards.back: To lead the homeowner to the best possible path for their specific situation.

cards.front: In the BMH mindset, what is more effective at moving a deal forward than any specific technique?
cards.back: Establishing trust by genuinely focusing on understanding the seller's needs.

cards.front: What is the fundamental shift in role for a BMH rep compared to a traditional salesperson?
cards.back: The rep acts as a consultant and guide rather than a high-pressure seller.

options.option_text: To give a clear path through every conversation.

When they need to listen, slow down, or be present with the seller.

questions.question_text: In the BMH mindset, what is more effective at moving a deal forward than any specific technique?

options.option_text: Assuming every person should choose BMH as the solution to their situation.

lessons.title: Real Estate Terms Glossary
lessons.description: Build the vocabulary needed to follow property, title, financing, and transaction conversations without guessing.

content.html:
What you will learn

Build the vocabulary needed to follow property, title, financing, and transaction conversations without guessing.

  • Define distressed, off-market, on-market, MLS, and listing terms
  • Distinguish wholesaling, assignment, and double-close concepts
  • Recognize subject-to and seller-financing structures
  • Ask for clarification when a term affects a seller

Learner guide

  • Terms are tools for understanding, not jargon to impress sellers
  • Translate technical language into plain English
  • Never guess about legal or title questions
  • Use the current deal record as the source for property facts

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Real Estate Terms Glossary

cards.front: A home in rough physical shape or owned by someone facing a difficult life situation is known as a _____.
cards.back: distressed property

cards.front: What is the primary objective of BMH Group when engaging with potential sellers?
cards.back: Identifying if the owner is in a distressed situation.

cards.front: What does it mean for a buyer to purchase a property 'as-is'?
cards.back: The buyer accepts the home in its current condition without requiring repairs, cleaning, or staging.

cards.front: A home that is not publicly listed for sale and has no real estate agent involved is an _____.
cards.back: off-market property

cards.front: What is the Multiple Listing Service (MLS)?
cards.back: A database used by licensed real estate agents to post and view homes for sale.

cards.front: Why does BMH Group generally avoid targeting on-market properties?
cards.back: Agent involvement and market pricing usually leave insufficient room for investment numbers to work.

cards.front: A property that was previously listed on the MLS but failed to sell is called an _____.
cards.back: expired listing

cards.front: In real estate listings, what does the acronym DOM represent?
cards.back: Days on Market (the length of time a property has been listed without selling).

quiz.title: Real Estate Terms Glossary Checkpoint

questions.question_text: A home in rough physical shape or owned by someone facing a difficult life situation is known as a _____.

lessons.title: Tech Stack and Systems
lessons.description: Understand where lead data, calls, research, practice, time tracking, and team communication belong.

content.html:
What you will learn

Understand where lead data, calls, research, practice, time tracking, and team communication belong.

  • Identify the purpose of each core tool
  • Keep Sandra as the lead source of truth
  • Use Closer Lab for deliberate practice
  • Escalate blocked work through the correct team channel

Learner guide

  • If lead activity is not recorded in Sandra it is not operationally visible
  • Use property tools for research and Sandra for relationship history
  • Complete assigned tasks and next actions on time
  • Ask your manager for the current SOP when a tool workflow changes

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: Humanizing the Lead
lessons.description: Recognize the people and pressures behind distressed-property data and determine whether BMH Group can genuinely help.

content.html:
What you will learn

Recognize the people and pressures behind distressed-property data and determine whether BMH Group can genuinely help.

  • Describe the ideal seller profile
  • Identify urgency, condition, equity, and decision-maker signals
  • Use respectful first-person language
  • Disqualify poor fits without dehumanizing the seller

Learner guide

  • A lead is a person in a real situation
  • Motivation is discovered through listening, not assumed from a list
  • Hard filters protect both the seller and the team
  • A seller's timeline and desired outcome matter as much as property data

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: The BMH Offer Playbook
lessons.description: Explain how a direct property purchase exchanges maximum retail price for speed, certainty, convenience, and an as-is sale.

content.html:
What you will learn

Explain how a direct property purchase exchanges maximum retail price for speed, certainty, convenience, and an as-is sale.

  • Explain the four offer pillars
  • Describe ARV and repair-cost inputs
  • Compare direct sale and traditional listing tradeoffs
  • Present the offer without promising an outcome

Learner guide

  • The offer is a solution with tradeoffs, not a claim to be the highest price
  • Use plain English when explaining the math
  • Confirm the seller values the benefits before positioning them
  • Do not invent property values or repair estimates

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for The BMH Offer Playbook

cards.front: What is the primary way BMH Group explains their offer to homeowners in 'plain English'?
cards.back: They buy houses for cash, as-is, with no repairs, agents, commissions, or waiting.

cards.front: Why does a cash offer provide more 'certainty' to a seller than a traditional bank-financed offer?
cards.back: It eliminates the risk of the deal falling through due to loan approval or underwriting failures.

cards.front: Traditional home sales often require an appraisal contingency; how does BMH Group handle this?
cards.back: They do not require an appraisal contingency because they do not use bank loans.

cards.front: What is the typical underwriting wait time for a bank loan that BMH Group avoids?
cards.back: 45 days.

cards.front: Under an 'as-is' agreement, who is responsible for handling major issues like foundation cracks or leaking roofs?
cards.back: BMH Group.

cards.front: What type of extreme property condition involving 'stuff piled to the ceiling' does the guide explicitly mention BMH Group handles?
cards.back: Hoarding situations.

cards.front: What percentage range of the sale price do sellers typically pay in real estate commissions during a traditional sale?
cards.back: 5 to 6 percent.

lessons.title: Sales Pipeline and Stage Ownership
lessons.description: Move leads through the pipeline with a clear purpose, complete notes, and an owner at every stage.

content.html:
What you will learn

Move leads through the pipeline with a clear purpose, complete notes, and an owner at every stage.

  • Describe the purpose of each pipeline stage
  • Identify the exit criteria for stages 1 through 4
  • Use the five-step conversation framework
  • Keep every active lead tied to a next action

Learner guide

  • A stage describes what is true now, not what you hope will happen
  • Do not advance a lead before the exit criteria are met
  • Document the reason for every stage change
  • The handoff begins only after qualification and discovery are complete

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Sales Pipeline and Stage Ownership

cards.front: How many total stages comprise the BMH Sales Pipeline?
cards.back: Six stages.

cards.front: What should happen when discovery confirms that a lead is not a fit or the person has clearly opted out?
cards.back: Disqualify the lead with the reason documented; an unanswered contact attempt alone should remain in follow-up instead.

cards.front: Which pipeline stages are owned and managed by the seller-facing representatives?
cards.back: Stages 1 through 4.

cards.front: Pipeline Stage 1: _____.
cards.back: Lead Capture

cards.front: What is the entry point for a lead in Stage 1 (Lead Capture)?
cards.back: A lead entering the system from a source like marketing, cold calling, or referrals.

cards.front: What information is typically available when a lead first enters Stage 1?
cards.back: Basics like name, phone number, property address, and minor source details.

cards.front: What is the representative's primary goal during Stage 1 (Lead Capture)?
cards.back: Make first contact and start a live two-way conversation with the property owner.

cards.front: What action must occur for a lead to exit Stage 1?
cards.back: A real, live, two-way conversation with the seller.

lessons.title: Opening the Call
lessons.description: Open seller conversations with permission, relevance, a clear frame, and a disciplined fact find.

content.html:
What you will learn

Open seller conversations with permission, relevance, a clear frame, and a disciplined fact find.

  • Use the opening frame without sounding scripted
  • Earn permission to continue
  • Capture essential property and seller facts
  • Transition from facts to deeper discovery

Learner guide

  • State why you are calling and give the seller room to respond
  • Use a calm pace and equal-status tone
  • Write down details instead of relying on memory
  • The fact find creates context. It does not replace discovery

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Opening the Call

lessons.title: Discovery and Handoff
lessons.description: Uncover the seller's situation, consequences, timeline, and decision process then deliver a clean acquisition handoff.

content.html:
What you will learn

Uncover the seller's situation, consequences, timeline, and decision process then deliver a clean acquisition handoff.

  • Distinguish qualification from discovery
  • Ask consequence and future-state questions
  • Confirm decision-makers and expectations
  • Complete a concise handoff with all required context

Learner guide

  • Discovery explains why the seller may act
  • Ask one question at a time and listen to the full answer
  • Do not diagnose or advise outside your role
  • A clean handoff lets the next person continue without making the seller repeat everything

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Discovery and Handoff

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the 'qualification' phase?
cards.back: Determining if it is possible to do business with the person based on basic criteria.

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the 'discovery' phase?
cards.back: Determining why the business should be done with the person right now.

cards.front: In the context of real estate leads, what does qualification confirm?
cards.back: Basic facts such as property ownership, residential status, and market location.

cards.front: In the context of real estate leads, what does discovery uncover?
cards.back: The seller's real motivation and underlying pain.

cards.front: Why is the first answer a seller gives about their reason for selling often insufficient?
cards.back: It is usually a surface-level or 'polished' answer rather than the real story.

cards.front: How should a representative ask 'why' questions without sounding like a child?
cards.back: By asking about drivers, timing, or what changed to make them think about selling now.

options.option_text: a specific acquisition appointment for the next call.
the acquisition manager's pressure-free evaluation.
basic property ownership and market-location facts.
the deeper layers of seller motivation.

Stage 2.
Stage 4.

Performing a warm transfer or setting a specific time for the next call.
Completing all CRM notes and documenting every detail from discovery.

options.option_text: Spouses, siblings, and attorneys involved in the decision.
Mortgage balance, property condition, and a specific appointment.
Tenant burnout, back taxes, and emotional attachment to an inherited property.
Property ownership, residential status, and market location.

questions.question_text: What is the third step in the acquisition handoff process?
questions.explanation: Performing a warm transfer or setting a specific appointment for the next call.

options.option_text: Framing the handoff so the seller knows exactly what to expect next.
Briefing the acquisition manager with the seller's story and real context.
Performing a warm transfer or setting a specific appointment for the next call.
Completing all CRM notes and documenting every detail from discovery.

questions.question_text: Why is it important to set a specific appointment time for the acquisition call?

options.option_text: Performing a warm transfer or setting a specific time for the next call.
Completing all CRM notes and documenting every detail from discovery.

questions.question_text: How should the acquisition manager's role be framed to the seller?
questions.explanation: As a pressure-free, no-obligation evaluation of the property to put together an offer.

options.option_text: As a pressure-free, no-obligation evaluation of the property to put together an offer.

options.option_text: Confidence that repeating the full discovery story is part of the next call.

questions.question_text: According to the Stage 4 checklist, what financial information must be noted?
questions.explanation: Mortgage balance and any liens or back taxes.

options.option_text: Seller motivation and emotional hot buttons.
Mortgage balance and any liens or back taxes.
Decision-makers and the acquisition appointment time.
Property condition and market location.

questions.question_text: What are 'hot buttons' in the context of a seller handoff?

options.option_text: Basic facts about ownership, residential status, and market location.
Financial figures such as mortgage balance, liens, and back taxes.
Handoff logistics such as the warm transfer and appointment time.
Sensitivities or specific emotional drivers that the acquisition manager needs to be aware of.

questions.question_text: What is a major cause of seller frustration during the handoff?
questions.explanation: Having to re-ask and re-answer questions they already covered with the first person.

options.option_text: Receiving a warm introduction before speaking with the acquisition manager.
Having the earlier discovery story documented in complete CRM notes.
Being told the specific time and expectations for the next acquisition call.

questions.question_text: What happens if an acquisition manager lacks context on a seller's motivation?
questions.explanation: They might accidentally step on an emotional 'landmine,' causing the seller to shut down.

options.option_text: The manager may treat a tentative price expectation as an accepted contract price.
The seller may receive questions meant for a title company instead of an acquisition evaluation.
They might accidentally step on an emotional 'landmine,' causing the seller to shut down.
The lead may be sent to transaction closing before an offer is reviewed.

questions.question_text: What is the negative result of failing to provide a warm introduction to the seller?
questions.explanation: The seller's guard goes back up when they receive a call from a stranger.

options.option_text: The seller expects the transaction team to close before an offer is reviewed.
The seller's guard goes back up when they receive a call from a stranger.
The seller believes the acquisition call creates a binding obligation.
The seller assumes the property was denied before the evaluation.

questions.question_text: What 'what if' timeline is suggested to force a seller to confront consequences?
questions.explanation: Six months from now.

options.option_text: One year from now.
Three months from now.
One month from now.

questions.question_text: How does discovery differ from qualification regarding motivation?
questions.explanation: Qualification confirms a reason exists, while discovery uncovers what is actually driving that reason.

options.option_text: Qualification confirms a reason exists, while discovery uncovers what is actually driving that reason.

questions.question_text: What property-specific information is required for a complete Stage 4 handoff?

options.option_text: A description of the property condition.

questions.explanation: It identifies the specific trigger or event that created the current urgency.

options.option_text: It identifies who has authority to sign off on the potential deal.
It frames the acquisition manager's evaluation and the next handoff step.
It identifies the specific trigger or event that created the current urgency.
It confirms ownership, residential status, and the property's market location.

assignment.title: Conversation and Handoff Plan
assignment.instructions: Draft a permission-based seller opening and the first five fact-find questions. Then draft five discovery questions and a handoff summary for a seller who is tired of managing repairs and tenant issues. Include the pipeline stage, known facts, missing facts, motivation, timeline, decision-makers, and next action.

rubric.description: Moves naturally from permission and facts into discovery.
Questions surface consequences, timing, priorities, and decision process.
The summary is accurate, concise, complete, and actionable.

modules.title: Objections and Questions
modules.description: Respond to concerns with empathy, structure, and accurate process information.

lessons.title: Objection Architecture
lessons.description: Use a repeatable framework to understand concerns before selecting a response.

content.html:
What you will learn

Use a repeatable framework to understand concerns before selecting a response.

  • Apply Listen, Acknowledge, Ask, Redirect
  • Classify common objection types
  • Avoid arguing with the seller
  • Choose a response that matches the concern

Learner guide

  • An objection is information before it is resistance
  • Acknowledge without automatically agreeing
  • Ask enough to identify the real concern
  • Redirect only after the seller feels heard

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Objection Architecture

cards.front: According to the text, what is the primary factor that distinguishes a top-performing caller from an average one?
cards.back: Their response when a seller pushes back or provides an objection.

cards.front: Why should objections be viewed as a positive sign in a real estate conversation?
cards.back: They indicate the seller is engaged rather than simply hanging up.

cards.back: They would say "no thanks" and hang up immediately.

cards.front: Instead of viewing an objection as a rejection, how should a caller rewire their thinking?
cards.back: As a conversation that still has momentum.

cards.front: When a seller pushes back, what should the caller's internal mindset be?
cards.back: Genuine curiosity about what is actually occurring.

cards.front: What are the four distinct types of responses a caller will encounter?
cards.back: Silence, complaints, reactionary defense responses, and real objections.

cards.front: What is the recommended response when a seller goes silent after you speak?
cards.back: Wait and give them space to process what was said.

cards.front: In a sales call, what usually happens to the person who speaks first after a period of silence?
cards.back: They give up ground.

quiz.title: Objection Architecture Checkpoint
quiz.description: Each attempt includes every question in the lesson pool in randomized order.

questions.question_text: According to the text, what is the primary factor that distinguishes a top-performing caller from an average one?
questions.explanation: Their response when a seller pushes back or provides an objection.

options.option_text: How quickly they deliver a rebuttal when the seller goes silent.
How many specific rebuttals they memorize before making calls.
Their willingness to agree with every concern the seller raises.
Their response when a seller pushes back or provides an objection.

questions.question_text: Why should objections be viewed as a positive sign in a real estate conversation?
questions.explanation: They indicate the seller is engaged rather than simply hanging up.

options.option_text: They mean the seller wants an immediate offer without any questions.
They indicate the seller is engaged rather than simply hanging up.
They show the seller has decided to list and only needs repairs assessed.
They prove the seller values speed more than a higher market price.

questions.question_text: If a seller truly had zero interest in selling, how would they likely respond instead of objecting?
questions.explanation: They would say "no thanks" and hang up immediately.

options.option_text: They would mention other offers and ask to compare the terms.
They would say "no thanks" and hang up immediately.
They would ask for the offer to be sent in writing.
They would remain silent while processing the caller's information.

questions.question_text: Instead of viewing an objection as a rejection, how should a caller rewire their thinking?
questions.explanation: As a conversation that still has momentum.

options.option_text: As a conversation that still has momentum.
As proof that the seller wants an immediate offer.
As a dead end that requires the call to end.
As permission to argue until the seller agrees.

questions.question_text: When a seller pushes back, what should the caller's internal mindset be?
questions.explanation: Genuine curiosity about what is actually occurring.

options.option_text: Genuine curiosity about what is actually occurring.
Determination to win the argument with a memorized rebuttal.
Confidence that the seller's concern should be dismissed.
Urgency to speed up before the seller ends the call.

questions.question_text: What are the four distinct types of responses a caller will encounter?
questions.explanation: Silence, complaints, reactionary defense responses, and real objections.

options.option_text: Listen, acknowledge, ask, and redirect.
Price, repairs, timeline, and trust.
Empathy, curiosity, certainty, and speed.
Silence, complaints, reactionary defense responses, and real objections.

questions.question_text: What is the recommended response when a seller goes silent after you speak?
questions.explanation: Wait and give them space to process what was said.

options.option_text: Speed up the process before the seller can object.
Wait and give them space to process what was said.
Fill the silence by explaining the offer in more detail.
Redirect immediately without waiting for their reaction.

questions.question_text: In a sales call, what usually happens to the person who speaks first after a period of silence?
questions.explanation: They give up ground.

options.option_text: They prove the seller is ready to accept.
They give up ground.
They identify the seller's real concern.
They gain leverage in the conversation.

questions.question_text: How does the text define "complaints" in the context of real estate calls?
questions.explanation: Frustrated venting from sellers who have likely received many investor calls.

options.option_text: A genuine concern that creates a specific barrier to a yes.
Frustrated venting from sellers who have likely received many investor calls.
A quiet pause while the seller processes what was said.
Automatic pushback meant to protect the seller's time or space.

questions.question_text: What are the three steps to handling a seller's complaint?
questions.explanation: Acknowledge the complaint, show empathy, and redirect the conversation.

options.option_text: Listen to the complaint, argue the facts, and send an offer.
Acknowledge the complaint, show empathy, and redirect the conversation.
Wait through the silence, explain the listing, and agree.
Ask for a number, speed up the process, and end the call.

questions.question_text: What is a reactionary defense response?
questions.explanation: Automatic, reflexive pushback meant to protect the seller's time or space, similar to saying 'just looking' in a store.

options.option_text: A quiet pause while the seller actively processes new information.
Automatic, reflexive pushback meant to protect the seller's time or space, similar to saying 'just looking' in a store.
A specific concern the seller has considered and needs resolved before saying yes.
Frustrated venting about repeated investor calls without a specific barrier.

questions.question_text: What are two common examples of reactionary defense responses?
questions.explanation: "You've got two minutes" or "Just make me an offer."

options.option_text: "I need to think about it" or "I want to list with a Realtor."
"The market is hot" or "I am getting other offers."
"You've got two minutes" or "Just make me an offer."
"Your price is too low" or "I need to talk to my spouse."

questions.question_text: What is the primary mistake to avoid when a seller uses a reactionary defense response?
questions.explanation: Speeding up the process because the seller told you to hurry.

options.option_text: Acknowledging the time constraint before continuing steadily.
Giving the seller space to finish their thought completely.
Speeding up the process because the seller told you to hurry.
Asking questions to understand the seller's actual concern.

questions.question_text: How should a caller handle a seller saying "you've got two minutes"?
questions.explanation: Acknowledge the time constraint and continue the process at a steady pace.

options.option_text: Treat the time constraint as a dead end and end the call.
Acknowledge the time constraint and continue the process at a steady pace.
Speed up and skip questions so the process fits the limit.
Argue that two minutes is not enough time for the conversation.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes a "real objection" from other types of pushback?
questions.explanation: It is a genuine concern that deserves a thoughtful, structured response.

options.option_text: It is a quiet pause while the seller processes what was said.
It is frustrated venting after the seller receives many investor calls.
It is automatic pushback intended to protect the seller's time or space.
It is a genuine concern that deserves a thoughtful, structured response.

questions.question_text: During the "Listen" step of the framework, what must the caller wait for?
questions.explanation: Wait for the seller to finish their thought completely before responding.

options.option_text: Wait only long enough to prepare the caller's memorized rebuttal.
Wait for the seller to ask for an immediate offer before responding.
Wait for the seller to finish their thought completely before responding.
Wait until the seller provides a specific price they will accept.

questions.question_text: What is the primary goal of the "Acknowledge" step in the objection framework?
questions.explanation: To show the seller you actually heard and understand their concern.

options.option_text: To show the seller that the caller automatically agrees with the concern.
To show the seller you actually heard and understand their concern.
To move toward an offer before the seller has explained the concern.
To prove that the caller has a stronger argument than the seller.

questions.question_text: In the four-step framework, what is the consequence of arguing with a seller?
questions.explanation: The caller immediately loses the opportunity to move the lead forward.

options.option_text: The seller feels heard and explains the concern more fully.
The caller immediately loses the opportunity to move the lead forward.
The conversation moves toward a mutually beneficial solution.
The caller gathers the information needed to solve the problem.

questions.question_text: What should a caller do in the third step of the framework instead of delivering a rebuttal?
questions.explanation: Ask a follow-up question to gather more information.

options.option_text: Redirect to a solution before gathering any details.
Restate the concern to demonstrate empathy.
Ask a follow-up question to gather more information.
Give the seller more space to repeat the same thought.

questions.question_text: How does gathering information through questions help in the objection framework?
questions.explanation: It provides the details necessary to actually solve the seller's problem.

options.option_text: It gives the caller permission to dismiss the seller's concern.
It provides the details necessary to actually solve the seller's problem.
It lets the caller skip acknowledging what the seller said.
It proves the seller has already accepted the proposed solution.

questions.question_text: What is the objective of the final step, "Redirect," in the framework?
questions.explanation: To guide the conversation toward a potential solution once the concern is understood.

options.option_text: To return to a memorized rebuttal instead of gathering information.
To guide the conversation toward a potential solution once the concern is understood.
To end the conversation once the concern has been identified.
To guide the seller toward a solution before they finish speaking.

questions.question_text: If a seller says "Your price is too low," what specific question should the caller ask?
questions.explanation: "What number were you hoping for?"

options.option_text: "What number were you hoping for?"
"How long has it been on the market?"
"Why did you respond to our outreach in the first place?"
"Can I ask what specifically you're weighing?"

questions.question_text: When a seller claims a price is too low, what are they often doing if they don't have a specific number in mind?
questions.explanation: They are testing the caller.

options.option_text: They are testing the caller.
They are rejecting every type of sale.
They are confirming their listing failed.
They are asking to end the call.

questions.question_text: What is the recommended question to ask when a seller says, "I need to think about it"?
questions.explanation: "Can I ask what specifically you're weighing?"

options.option_text: "Can I ask what specifically you're weighing?"
"How long has the property been on the market?"
"What have you seen so far from the other offers?"
"What number were you hoping for?"

questions.question_text: What two factors should you ask a seller about if they want to list with a Realtor?
questions.explanation: Repair costs needed for listing and the ability to wait several months for a sale.

options.option_text: The reason for answering the outreach and the need for a fast sale.
The licensed title company and the availability of an attorney review.
The other offer structures and the numbers the seller has seen so far.
Repair costs needed for listing and the ability to wait several months for a sale.

questions.question_text: How should a caller respond when a seller mentions they are getting other offers?
questions.explanation: Encourage them to explore options and ask what they have seen so far to ensure an "apples to apples" comparison.

options.option_text: Encourage them to explore options and ask what they have seen so far to ensure an "apples to apples" comparison.
Tell them the other offers are unreliable and argue that yours is better.
Treat the mention of another offer as a dead end and end the call.
Ask them to stop exploring options and accept the current offer instead.

questions.question_text: What should a caller do before agreeing to send an offer or information in writing?
questions.explanation: Ask questions to ensure the information sent is actually relevant to the seller's situation.

options.option_text: Ask questions to ensure the information sent is actually relevant to the seller's situation.
Ask the seller to list the property before providing the information.
Explain why the seller should accept before asking any questions.
Send it immediately so the caller does not lose ground during silence.

questions.question_text: What is the suggested follow-up for a seller who says they are "not interested"?
questions.explanation: Ask why they responded to the initial outreach in the first place.

options.option_text: Ask what number the seller was hoping to receive.
Ask what the seller has seen from other offers so far.
Ask whether the seller can wait several months for a sale.
Ask why they responded to the initial outreach in the first place.

questions.question_text: How should a caller explain how they obtained a seller's phone number?
questions.explanation: Explain that the property matched buying criteria in their system for homeowners who might benefit from a fast sale.

options.option_text: Explain that the property matched buying criteria in their system for homeowners who might benefit from a fast sale.
Explain that the seller answered the outreach and requested a fast sale.
Explain that the number came from another offer being compared with theirs.
Explain that the property was already listed and the caller is the backup option.

questions.question_text: How should a caller build trust with a seller who says, "I don't trust investors"?
questions.explanation: Mention working with licensed title companies, proper closings, and attorney reviews.

options.option_text: Speed up the call and avoid discussing why the seller feels skeptical.
Promise a higher market price without explaining the closing process.
Mention working with licensed title companies, proper closings, and attorney reviews.
Tell the seller that a hot market should be enough reason to trust investors.

questions.question_text: What value proposition should be emphasized when a seller says the "market is hot"?
questions.explanation: Certainty and speed, including no repairs or deals falling through.

options.option_text: A backup listing plan that applies only after the seller's agent fails.
An immediate offer delivered before the caller understands the concern.
Certainty and speed, including no repairs or deals falling through.
A potentially higher price after repairs and several months of waiting.

questions.question_text: What is the suggested "backup plan" script when a seller already has an agent?
questions.explanation: Ask how long it has been on the market and suggest they keep your number in case the listing fails.

options.option_text: Tell the seller to stop exploring options until the caller follows up.
Argue that using an agent means the seller cannot receive a fair offer.
Ask how long it has been on the market and suggest they keep your number in case the listing fails.
Ask the seller to cancel the listing and accept an immediate offer.

questions.question_text: What is the one thing a caller should "absolutely never do" with an objection?
questions.explanation: Treat it like a dead end.

options.option_text: Use it as a doorway into a deeper conversation.
Treat it like a dead end.
Treat it as information that should be understood.
Apply the four-step framework to the concern.

questions.question_text: Instead of "overcoming" objections as if in a battle, what is the caller's actual job?
questions.explanation: To understand the objections.

options.option_text: To memorize specific rebuttals.
To understand the objections.
To argue until the seller agrees.
To speed through every objection.

questions.question_text: What metaphorical description does the text use for an objection?
questions.explanation: A doorway into a deeper conversation.

options.option_text: A signal that the caller should speed up.
A battle the caller must win with a rebuttal.
A doorway into a deeper conversation.
A dead end that closes the conversation.

questions.question_text: When handling a complaint, what is the purpose of saying "I want to make sure this call is actually worth your time"?
questions.explanation: To provide a professional redirect that justifies the conversation.

options.option_text: To rebut the seller's frustration by proving that it is incorrect.

lessons.title: Objection Scripts Playbook
lessons.description: Practice adaptable responses for price, timing, trust, competition, and decision-maker concerns.

content.html:
What you will learn

Practice adaptable responses for price, timing, trust, competition, and decision-maker concerns.

  • Select a script pattern that matches the objection
  • Adapt wording without changing its intent
  • Use questions to reopen dialogue
  • Know when to pause and seek guidance

Learner guide

  • Scripts are guardrails, not lines to recite at any cost
  • Use the seller's language when reflecting a concern
  • Do not answer a question you do not understand
  • A graceful exit protects trust when there is no fit

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Objection Scripts Playbook

cards.front: What are the four components of the objection-handling framework used in the BMH training?
cards.back: Listen, acknowledge, ask, and redirect.

cards.front: According to the playbook, what is the primary purpose of drilling real objections repeatedly?
cards.back: To build muscle memory so the real objections are easier to handle.

cards.front: If a seller says 'I want more money,' what specific closing question should follow the offer to check for more room?
cards.back: Are you ready to sign and move forward today?

cards.front: Why does the playbook advise against giving your price first on a call?
cards.back: The seller might shop your number and reject you before you even see the house.

cards.front: When a seller claims they don't know their price, what 'imagination' prompt does the playbook suggest?
cards.back: If you truly don't know, imagine you did know-what would that look like?

cards.front: What analogy involving a car is used to encourage a seller to provide a starting price?
cards.back: The Craigslist analogy: you wouldn't list a car for $0 and say 'make me an offer.'

cards.front: Under what two conditions is listing with an agent considered a 'great path' for a seller?
cards.back: If the seller has time and the house is list-ready.

cards.front: What two choices should an investor present to a seller considering an agent?
cards.back: Highest possible price versus certainty and timeline.

lessons.title: Complex Objections
lessons.description: Handle emotionally or structurally difficult situations with patience, boundaries, and appropriate escalation.

content.html:
What you will learn

Handle emotionally or structurally difficult situations with patience, boundaries, and appropriate escalation.

  • Recognize complex ownership and family dynamics
  • Respond to scam and privacy concerns
  • Separate empathy from legal advice
  • Escalate cases that require specialist guidance

Learner guide

  • Slow down when the situation involves grief, conflict, or financial distress
  • Never pretend to be an attorney or financial adviser
  • Confirm who can legally make decisions
  • Document sensitivities so the next team member can respond appropriately

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: Seller FAQ Decoder
lessons.description: Answer common seller questions through the three lenses of trust, fairness, and simplicity.

content.html:
What you will learn

Answer common seller questions through the three lenses of trust, fairness, and simplicity.

  • Identify the concern behind a common question
  • Explain the direct-sale tradeoff
  • Answer clearly without overpromising
  • Return to the seller's stated priorities

Learner guide

  • Answer the question that was asked before adding detail
  • Use specific process facts instead of vague reassurance
  • If an answer varies by deal, say so
  • Confirm whether the answer resolved the seller's concern

assignment.title: Objection Response Plan

modules.title: Cadence, Scripts, and Close
modules.description: Follow up consistently, guide conversation flow, and create a clean next step.

lessons.title: Follow-Up Cadence
lessons.description: Use planned, respectful follow-up to stay present without turning persistence into pressure.

content.html:
What you will learn

Use planned, respectful follow-up to stay present without turning persistence into pressure.

  • Apply the follow-up cadence
  • Choose a relevant reason for each contact
  • Record every attempt and next action
  • Recognize when to pause or stop outreach

Learner guide

  • Every follow-up should add context or make the next step easier
  • Honor opt-outs and communication preferences
  • Use multiple approved channels when appropriate
  • Consistency matters more than one aggressive burst

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: Conversation Flow Mastery
lessons.description: Keep difficult conversations moving through thought experiments, option framing, price exploration, and respectful repositioning.

content.html:
What you will learn

Keep difficult conversations moving through thought experiments, option framing, price exploration, and respectful repositioning.

  • Use thought experiments to create clarity
  • Frame available paths without false urgency
  • Explore price expectations
  • Re-engage or de-position gracefully

Learner guide

  • Offer choices only when each choice is real
  • Use curiosity to test assumptions
  • Do not trap a seller into defending a number
  • A clean de-position can preserve a future relationship

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: Closing and Deal Engineering
lessons.description: Prepare and present an offer as a clear next step while protecting accuracy, consent, and handoff quality.

content.html:
What you will learn

Prepare and present an offer as a clear next step while protecting accuracy, consent, and handoff quality.

  • Prepare the information needed for an offer
  • Present terms cleanly
  • Identify unresolved decision barriers
  • Confirm the next action without pressure

Learner guide

  • A strong close starts with accurate discovery
  • State what is known and what still requires confirmation
  • Let the seller evaluate the tradeoff
  • Document the decision and exact next step

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Closing and Deal Engineering

cards.front: In a successful sales process, why should the closing stage ideally be the easiest part?
cards.back: Because the rapport, discovery, and objection handling have already been completed successfully.

cards.front: By the time the closing stage is reached, how has the seller typically already decided about the deal?
cards.back: They have decided to do business with the person they like and trust.

cards.back: formal agreement

cards.front: Which specific team is responsible for presenting the offer and negotiating terms?
cards.back: The acquisition team.

cards.back: It provides the specific information and documentation the team needs to structure the offer.

cards.front: Why is it important for a representative to understand how the closing process works?
cards.back: It allows them to help with follow-ups and perform better discovery for the acquisition team.

cards.back: Deal Sniper and comparable sales data.

cards.front: How should an offer be presented to a seller to ensure it is effective?
cards.back: It should be clean, simple, and presented without pressure or gimmicks.

lessons.title: KPIs and Sales Telemetry
lessons.description: Read the funnel from left to right to locate process gaps and choose the right coaching response.

content.html:
What you will learn

Read the funnel from left to right to locate process gaps and choose the right coaching response.

  • Define the six core metrics
  • Separate activity from productive progress
  • Diagnose where a funnel breaks
  • Use metrics for coaching rather than punishment

Learner guide

  • Metrics show where to investigate. They do not explain everything by themselves
  • Use the current role-and-market scorecard. Training does not set one universal numeric target
  • Check conversion between adjacent stages
  • Pair numbers with notes and call review before deciding on a fix

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

lessons.title: Compensation Engine
lessons.description: Understand that compensation depends on the value a role owns and that the current written plan is always the source of truth.

content.html:
What you will learn

Understand that compensation depends on the value a role owns and that the current written plan is always the source of truth.

  • Identify the current compensation source of truth
  • Connect role outcomes to the role scorecard
  • Know where to take compensation questions
  • Avoid relying on another person's plan or an old example

Learner guide

  • There is no universal formula for every role
  • Use your current offer letter, written plan, or role sheet
  • Ask your manager when any part of the plan is unclear
  • Do not treat training examples or conversations as a compensation promise

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Compensation Engine

cards.front: Which description best separates compensation training from an employee's actual compensation terms?
cards.back: Training explains the compensation components; the current written plan supplies the employee's binding terms.

cards.front: What business purpose can ramp-period pay serve?
cards.back: It provides income while the employee learns the role and develops a pipeline, subject to the plan.

cards.front: A training example shows one ramp-pay amount. What may the learner safely conclude?
cards.back: The example illustrates a concept; the current plan controls the actual terms.

cards.front: Which evidence should a reviewer use to decide whether an employee has completed the ramp period?
cards.back: The applicable ramp criteria plus documented results for the employee's measured review period.

cards.front: What makes a transition from ramp compensation to a post-ramp structure valid?
cards.back: Meeting the criteria and confirming the transition under the current plan.

cards.front: Which record best supports a compensation-eligibility review for work on a lead?
cards.back: A traceable CRM history showing sourcing, qualification, follow-up, and handoff activity.

cards.front: After a seller-facing representative qualifies a lead and completes the handoff, which team owns negotiation and closing?
cards.back: The acquisition team receiving the qualified lead handoff.

cards.front: Which facts are needed before a per-deal commission can be calculated?
cards.back: The deal's eligibility and timing records plus the applicable commission-rate and tier terms.

lessons.title: Operator Playbook and Daily Mission Control
lessons.description: Run a disciplined workday, keep the pipeline current, and communicate decisions and blockers through the team's operating systems.

content.html:
What you will learn

Run a disciplined workday, keep the pipeline current, and communicate decisions and blockers through the team's operating systems.

  • Prioritize daily lead work
  • Maintain notes, stages, and next actions
  • Use team communication channels professionally
  • Close the day with a clean pipeline and clear plan

Learner guide

  • Begin with scheduled and high-priority follow-ups
  • Log notes immediately after meaningful activity
  • Ask rather than guess when a situation is unfamiliar
  • A worked day reflects complete controllable actions, not a guaranteed outcome

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

content.description: Accessible learner guide for Operator Playbook and Daily Mission Control

lessons.title: Career Growth Path
lessons.description: Build capability in your current role through deliberate practice, useful feedback, coachability, and reliable execution.

content.html:
What you will learn

Build capability in your current role through deliberate practice, useful feedback, coachability, and reliable execution.

  • Use a practice and feedback loop
  • Respond to coaching without defensiveness
  • Connect capability to current role expectations
  • Confirm any increase in ownership with your manager and current written role plan

Learner guide

  • Practice, apply feedback, and review the result
  • Capability means using knowledge and skills reliably in current work
  • Your manager and current written role plan define your responsibilities and expectations
  • Take on increased ownership only after the change is documented in your current written role plan

Use the current written SOP and ask your manager when a live process differs from this lesson.

assignment.title: Mission Control and Growth Capstone
assignment.instructions: Build a one-day operating plan with priorities, checkpoints, metrics, pipeline hygiene, team communication, and end-of-day review. Add a short reflection on one skill to improve, how you will measure it, and how you will use coaching. Finish with two written case debriefs: how you would preserve authority and neutrality when an adult child opposes a homeowner's decision, and what you would carry forward through a complete seller conversation from opening to handoff.

rubric.description: The day protects follow-ups, documentation, communication, breaks, and review.
Chooses metrics that reveal a specific process gap without inventing targets.
Names a concrete practice and feedback loop tied to the current role.
Explains how the written case responses preserve consent, decision authority, neutrality, and an accurate handoff.
